section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: 23 October 2025

section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
Introduction
paragraphs.text: Gen, the global leader in consumer cybersecurity, fully supports the European Commission's initiatives to enhance online fairness for consumers. We share the Commission's commitment to fostering a transparent and empowering digital environment. To achieve this, the primary challenges of protecting consumers from genuine harm must be addressed while also ensuring legal certainty for businesses and avoiding market fragmentation across the Single Market.

section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
Introduction
paragraphs.text: This position paper outlines Gen's perspective on the forthcoming Digital Fairness Act, structured around three core principles we believe are fundamental to achieving a future-proof framework:

section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
Introduction
paragraphs.text: Recognising continuity of service as essential for consumer protection, particularly in the cybersecurity domain where service lapses may create consumer harm.

section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
Introduction
paragraphs.text: Promoting genuine consumer choice by clearly distinguishing between harmful 'dark patterns' and beneficial 'choice architecture' that helps users make informed decisions.

section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
Introduction
paragraphs.text: Prioritise effective enforcement and harmonised interpretation of existing consumer protection laws (UCPD, DSA, etc.) over creating new overlapping rules.

section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
Pillar 1: Continuity of “Essential Cybersecurity Services” is central to consumer protection
paragraphs.text: Online scams driven by generative AI are rising dramatically in the EU. In 2024, almost half of EU consumers experienced online fraud, causing massive financial losses: Germans may lose €10 billion in 2025, French citizens €7.6 billion, and Danish consumers over DKK 6.9 billion (over €1 billion).

section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
Pillar 1: Continuity of “Essential Cybersecurity Services” is central to consumer protection
paragraphs.text: At the same time, data breaches keep increasing. With events up 36% between Q4 2024 and Q1 2025, and compromised records (including emails, passwords, and credit card numbers) surging by 186%².

section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
Pillar 1: Continuity of “Essential Cybersecurity Services” is central to consumer protection
paragraphs.text: Essential cybersecurity services (antivirus, anti-malware, anti-scam, identity protection, etc.) should be treated as essential utilities in the modern digital home. Just

section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
Pillar 1: Continuity of “Essential Cybersecurity Services” is central to consumer protection
paragraphs.text: as consumers expect electricity or water to flow without interruption, they reasonably expect their devices and data to be continuously protected against cyber threats. A lapse of even a single day in antivirus or cybersecurity coverage can expose individuals and families to severe risks including malware infections, ransomware attacks, identity theft, and financial fraud.

section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
Pillar 1: Continuity of “Essential Cybersecurity Services” is central to consumer protection
paragraphs.text: If a security subscription quietly expires because a user overlooked a renewal notice, that consumer can unknowingly become vulnerable to these threats overnight.

section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
Pillar 1: Continuity of “Essential Cybersecurity Services” is central to consumer protection
paragraphs.text: Automatic renewal in this context functions as a critical safety net; it ensures there is no inadvertent gap in the user's defence. Rather than a mere commercial tactic, auto-renewal for cybersecurity is fundamentally a protection mechanism that sustains an ongoing shield against digital harm.

section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
Pillar 1: Continuity of “Essential Cybersecurity Services” is central to consumer protection
paragraphs.text: By treating cybersecurity as an essential service, the law would align with consumers' reasonable expectations of safety for their digital lives.

section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
Pillar 1: Continuity of “Essential Cybersecurity Services” is central to consumer protection
paragraphs.text: Gen recommends:

section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
Pillar 1: Continuity of “Essential Cybersecurity Services” is central to consumer protection
paragraphs.text: Differentiating high-risk consequences between subscriptions where lapse mainly results in inconvenience or missed content, versus those where lapse results in loss of security or safety. This differentiation echoes the principle in offline consumer protection that certain services warrant special continuation safeguards.

section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
Pillar 1: Continuity of “Essential Cybersecurity Services” is central to consumer protection
paragraphs.text: This protective function, however, is only legitimate when paired with a subscription management process that is equally transparent and empowers the consumer with an easy, frictionless way to cancel auto-renewal.

section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
Pillar 1: Continuity of “Essential Cybersecurity Services” is central to consumer protection
paragraphs.text: Pillar 2: Empowering consumer choice in subscription management

section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
Pillar 1: Continuity of “Essential Cybersecurity Services” is central to consumer protection
paragraphs.text: We fully agree with the Commission's objective to prevent "dark patterns" and "difficult cancellations" that manipulate consumers. The Fitness Check correctly notes that in the online context, consumers "are less likely to pay attention" and can be more easily swayed by "deceptive... interface designs." This is precisely why a distinction must be drawn between malicious dark patterns and a transparent "choice architecture" designed to help a consumer navigate complex choices and make a decision that best suits their needs, rather than one they might later regret. At Gen, our subscription management flows are designed not to create barriers, but to empower users with this exact flexibility and control.

section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
Pillar 1: Continuity of “Essential Cybersecurity Services” is central to consumer protection
paragraphs.text: Fair and transparent subscription management should not create barriers; it should empower users with flexibility and control. When a consumer indicates an intention to unsubscribe, a fair process treats this as an opportunity to ensure their changing needs are met. This involves presenting relevant, value-driven alternatives... alongside a clear and simple path to proceed with cancellation.

section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
Pillar 1: Continuity of “Essential Cybersecurity Services” is central to consumer protection
paragraphs.text: Notably, existing EU law already provides a baseline: the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive outlaws practices that materially distort consumer decisions, and the Digital

section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
Pillar 1: Continuity of “Essential Cybersecurity Services” is central to consumer protection
paragraphs.text: Services Act (DSA) explicitly forbids online platforms from designing interfaces that distort or impair users' ability to make free choices (Article 25).

section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
Pillar 1: Continuity of “Essential Cybersecurity Services” is central to consumer protection
paragraphs.text: Gen recommends:

section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
Pillar 1: Continuity of “Essential Cybersecurity Services” is central to consumer protection
paragraphs.text: Definition of "dark patterns" should be precise and focused on practices that are genuinely deceptive or manipulative, causing material harm as defined in the UCPD and DSA frameworks, rather than encompassing "choice architecture" made to present relevant alternatives.

section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
Pillar 1: Continuity of “Essential Cybersecurity Services” is central to consumer protection
paragraphs.text: We should avoid prescriptive, one-size-fits-all rules for user interfaces as this could inadvertently prohibit pro-consumer choice architecture that allows for flexible options like pausing or downgrading a subscription.

section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
Pillar 1: Continuity of “Essential Cybersecurity Services” is central to consumer protection
paragraphs.text: Pillar 3: Prioritising enforcement and guidance of existing legislation

section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
Pillar 1: Continuity of “Essential Cybersecurity Services” is central to consumer protection
paragraphs.text: The Commission's Fitness Check on consumer law regarding digital fairness provided an important finding: the greatest opportunity for enhancing digital fairness is ensuring the existing, robust legal framework is applied with greater clarity and more effective, harmonised enforcement. Over the past decade, the EU has established a comprehensive array of consumer protections, ranging from the UCPD and CRD to more recent regulations such as the DSA and DMA. Collectively, these instruments address most relevant issues; for instance, at least 13 pieces of EU legislation currently cover dark patterns and manipulative practices. Nevertheless, stakeholders have indicated uncertainty concerning the interplay among these rules, contributing to a complex and overlapping legal environment. The Fitness Check identified no significant gaps in the scope of consumer protection objectives but did highlight difficulties in applying broad existing principles to emerging digital practices and inconsistencies in implementation across Member States.

section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
Pillar 1: Continuity of “Essential Cybersecurity Services” is central to consumer protection
paragraphs.text: Therefore, focusing on clear guidance and harmonised enforcement of these existing rules represents the most direct and rapid path to delivering tangible improvements for consumers, avoiding the lengthy delays inherent in drafting, debating, and transposing entirely new legislation.

section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
Pillar 1: Continuity of “Essential Cybersecurity Services” is central to consumer protection
paragraphs.text: Gen strongly believes that the Commission's emphasis should be on reducing legal uncertainty and facilitating more effective enforcement of the existing legal framework. The most effective path to achieving digital fairness is to ensure the rules we already have are understood and applied uniformly and not increase confusion for everyone: consumers, businesses, and regulators.

section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
Pillar 1: Continuity of “Essential Cybersecurity Services” is central to consumer protection
paragraphs.text: To illustrate the importance of getting enforcement right, consider that some Member States have already started national initiatives against dark patterns (France, for example, has begun to define and police "manipulative design" under its consumer law; Norway's consumer authority issued reports on defaults and dark patterns, etc.). These

section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
Pillar 1: Continuity of “Essential Cybersecurity Services” is central to consumer protection
paragraphs.text: are valuable efforts, but if an EU law intervenes, it should synthesize and lead these efforts, not conflict with them.

section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
Gen recommends:
paragraphs.text: The Commission made a good step in 2021 by updating interpretative Guidance Notices for the UCPD and CRD to explain how they apply online. Still, there is room for **more detailed guidance or codes of conduct** on specific practices (for instance, guidelines on what constitutes a not too difficult cancellation process). Gen stands ready to contribute our expertise to the development of such practical guidelines to ensure they are effective and technologically relevant.

section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
Gen recommends:
paragraphs.text: Uniform consumer protection across the EU requires that national authorities work in concert when addressing big online players. The CPC Regulation already provides a framework for coordinated actions. However, the Fitness Check notes several challenges: lengthy procedures, varying resources/expertise among national authorities, difficulty in jointly imposing penalties, and chasing traders outside the EU. We support the Commission’s ongoing reflections on updating the CPC Regulation to address these issues.

section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
Gen recommends:
paragraphs.text: By focusing on enforcement and guidance, the proposal can **deliver clarity and predictability for businesses and consumers**. The aim should be **harmonisation**. This will create the “level playing field” that the Commission rightly seeks, benefiting both consumers (who get equal protection everywhere) and businesses (who face the same rules everywhere).

section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
Conclusion
paragraphs.text: To achieve the shared objective of a truly fair and empowering digital Single Market, Gen believes the forthcoming Digital Fairness Act should be built on three core pillars. A successful framework will:

section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
Conclusion
paragraphs.text: Recognise the essential protective function of service continuity for critical services like cybersecurity, safeguarding consumers from significant and often irreversible harm.

section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
Conclusion
paragraphs.text: Promote genuine consumer choice by clearly distinguishing manipulative 'dark patterns' from beneficial 'choice architecture' that supports transparent and informed decision-making.

section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
Conclusion
paragraphs.text: Prioritise the most effective path to fairness by focusing on the harmonised guidance and rigorous enforcement of the EU’s existing and robust legal acquis.

section_path: Gen Position Paper on Digital Fairness Act
Conclusion
paragraphs.text: This approach will bring clear benefits to consumers quickly and ensure legal certainty for a competitive, innovative market. Gen remains dedicated to these goals and looks forward to supporting the European Commission in developing an effective and future-ready Digital Fairness Act for all Europeans.

section_path: About Gen
paragraphs.text: At Gen, our mission is to create innovative and easy-to-use technology solutions that help people grow, manage, and secure their digital and financial lives. Dual headquartered in Prague, Czech Republic and Tempe, Arizona, Gen is a global company dedicated to powering Digital Freedom through its trusted brands including Norton, Avast, LifeLock, MoneyLion and more. Our family of consumer brands is rooted in providing financial empowerment and cybersafety for the first digital generations. Today, Gen empowers people to live their digital lives safely, privately and confidently for generations to come. Gen brings award-winning products and services in cybersecurity, online privacy, identity protection and financial wellness to nearly 500 million users in more than 150 countries. This scale gives us unparalleled, real-time data on the evolving threat landscape and positions our solutions as a critical preventive layer in the fight against cross-platform online scams.
